'''Brendon Ephia Tuuta'''<ref name="TUUTA, BRENDON EPHIA 1989, 1992 – 95 – KIWI #608">{{cite web|url=http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=T&id=608|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120402171121/http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=T&id=608|url-status=dead|archive-date=2 April 2012|title=TUUTA, BRENDON EPHIA 1989, 1992 – 95 – KIWI #608|publisher=nzleague.co.nz|date=2 April 2012|access-date=1 January 2013}}</ref> (born 29 April 1965) is a New Zealand former [[rugby league]] footballer of [[Māori people|Māori]] ([[Ngāti Mutunga]]) and [[Moriori people|Moriori]] descent.

Tuuta played a variety of positions including {{rlp|so|sh|sr}} and {{rlp|lf}}. He was known as "the baby-faced assassin" and had a reputation as a brawler.<ref name="Your Favorite Moments">{{cite web|url=http://www.eraofthebiff.com/p-fm21.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080807124450/http://www.eraofthebiff.com/p-fm21.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=7 August 2008|title=Your Favorite Moments|publisher=eraofthebiff.com|date=7 August 2008|access-date=1 January 2009}}</ref>

==Background==
He is related to [[Lewis Brown (rugby league)|Lewis Brown]].<ref name="New Warrior leaps to aid of snatch-and-run victim">{{cite web|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/808551/New-Warrior-leaps-to-aid-of-snatch-and-run-victim|title=New Warrior leaps to aid of snatch-and-run victim|date=24 January 2009|work= [[The Sunday Star-Times]]|access-date=21 September 2011}}</ref>

==Early years==
Originally from the [[Chatham Islands]], Tuuta played much of his youth league for [[Canterbury Rugby League|Canterbury]].

Tuuta withdrew from the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] squad for the [[1986 Pacific Cup]].<ref>John Coffey, Bernie Wood. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=nklWo8vw-iIC&dq=Brendon+Tuuta&pg=PT258 100 Years: Maori Rugby League, 1908–2008]''. Auckland, Huia Publishers, 2008. {{ISBN|1-86969-331-0}}</ref>

==Playing career==
Tuuta first played professionally when he played for the [[Western Suburbs Magpies]] in the [[New South Wales Rugby League premiership]] between 1989 and 1990.<ref name="Ask a tricky question and get some Tricky answers">{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/articles/2002/08/29/1030508100331.html|title=Ask a tricky question and get some Tricky answers|publisher=smh.com.au|date=30 August 2002|access-date=1 January 2003}}</ref>

He was a [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] international between 1989 and 1995. During his début in 1989 he was involved in an incident where it was claimed he kneed [[Paul Vautin]].<ref name="Tuuta Comes Out Of His Cocoon With A Halo">{{cite web|url=http://www.rotorua.com.au/rotorua-news/1989/7/14/tuuta-comes-out-of-his-cocoon-with-a-halo|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090913115631/http://www.rotorua.com.au/rotorua-news/1989/7/14/tuuta-comes-out-of-his-cocoon-with-a-halo/|url-status=dead|archive-date=13 September 2009|title=Tuuta Comes Out Of His Cocoon With A Halo|publisher=rotorua.com.au|date=14 July 1989|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref> He went on the [[1989 New Zealand rugby league tour|1989 Kiwis tour of Great Britain and France]] and played in Featherstone for the first time.<ref name="Rovers v New Zealand 1989: Our Introduction To Brendon Tuuta">{{cite web|url=http://www.fevshop.com/rovers-v-new-zealand-1989-our-introduction-to-brendon-tuuta-114-p.asp|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160123124302/http://www.fevshop.com/rovers-v-new-zealand-1989-our-introduction-to-brendon-tuuta-114-p.asp|url-status=dead|archive-date=23 January 2016|title=Rovers v New Zealand 1989: Our Introduction To Brendon Tuuta|publisher=fevshop.com|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

He then moved to [[Featherstone Rovers]] in England, playing in the second division.<ref name="Leeds have to tame Tuuta">{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/leeds-have-to-tame-tuuta-1613435.html|title=Leeds have to tame Tuuta|publisher=independent.co.uk|date=30 March 1995|access-date=1 January 2018}}{{dead link|date=August 2021|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> In 1993 he was suspended for six matches for breaking [[Nigel Heslop]]'s jaw with a punch.<ref name="Tuuta out for six matches">{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-tuuta-out-for-six-matches-1498661.html|title=Tuuta out for six matches|publisher=independent.co.uk|date=19 March 1993|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref> He played {{rlp|lf}} in the Rovers' 20–16 victory over [[Workington Town]] in the [[1992–93 Rugby Football League season|1992–93]] [[Rugby Football League Championship Second Division|Division Two Premiership]] Final at [[Old Trafford]], [[Manchester]] on 19 May 1993.<ref name="Rovers Dig Deep To Lift The 1993 RL Premiership">{{cite web|url=http://www.btinternet.com/~Rovers/gpgg11.htm|title=Rovers Dig Deep To Lift The 1993 RL Premiership|publisher=Great Players – Great Games|date=2010-12-31|access-date=2011-01-01|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121011225606/http://www.btinternet.com/~Rovers/gpgg11.htm|archive-date=2012-10-11}}</ref>

Tuuta was a [[Canterbury rugby league team|Canterbury]] representative and famously returned to New Zealand for the 1993 provincial grand final where Canterbury upset [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]], earning the man of the match award that day.<ref name="Stuff.co.nz_2458687">{{cite web|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/the-press/sport/2458687/Nothing-better-than-bettering-Auckland|title=Nothing better than bettering Auckland|author=Smith, Tony|date=30 May 2009|work= [[The Press]]|access-date=21 September 2011}}</ref>

During the 1995 Australian season, he returned to play for the [[WA Reds|Western Reds]],<ref name="Perth / Western Reds">{{cite web|url=http://www.rl1908.com/Clubs/Western-Reds-Perth.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20020621185507/http://rl1908.com/Clubs/Western-Reds-Perth.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=21 June 2002|title=Perth / Western Reds|publisher=rl1908.com|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref> before signing for the [[Castleford Tigers]] for the English 1995/96 season.<ref name="Profile at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=232|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120227102901/http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=232|url-status=dead|archive-date=27 February 2012|title=Profile at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|publisher=thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref> 

Tuuta joined the [[Warrington Wolves]] for the 1998 season but struggled with knee problems.<ref name="Tuuta Retires">{{cite web|url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/news/5310222.tuuta-retires/|title=Tuuta Retires|website=Warrington Guardian|date=2 October 1998|access-date=11 March 2026|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260311173821/https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/news/5310222.tuuta-retires/|archive-date=11 March 2026}}</ref> Despite deciding to retire after 1998 he reconsidered and returned to Featherstone Rovers for one more season.<ref name="Fearsome Fev for Ford fiesta">{{cite web|url=http://www.thefreelibrary.com/Rugby+League:+Fearsome+Fev+for+Ford+fiesta-a061505113|title=Fearsome Fev for Ford fiesta|publisher=The Racing Post at thefreelibrary.com|date=6 February 1999|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

==Honours==
Tuuta is a Featherstone Rovers' Hall of Fame inductee.<ref name="Featherstone Rovers legends added to Hall of Fame">{{cite web|url=http://www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk/news/featherstone-rovers-legends-added-to-hall-of-fame-1-8214653|title=Featherstone Rovers legends added to Hall of Fame|publisher=pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk|date=5 November 2016|access-date=1 January 2017}}</ref>
